articles.title: Partnerships & Stakeholders: Science and Technology Community
articles.description: The role of global science, academia, research and innovation is enshrined in the Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction where it is recognized for the catalytic, boundary-pushing and transformative potential it holds for addressing the dynamic drivers of disaster risk.

articles.title: Accelerating local disaster risk reduction in the Caribbean through the work of Making Cities Resilient 2030
articles.description: As global challenges grow in complexity and uncertainty, a coordinated, multilevel approach to international cooperation is essential to support cities and municipalities in addressing systemic risks that cross geographic and sectoral boundaries.

articles.title: Sendai Framework Focal Points and National Platforms
articles.description: By ensuring effective coordination of disaster risk reduction at national level, National Platforms play an instrumental role in ensuring an all-of-government and all-of-society approach to reducing risk and building resilience. These efforts are intimately tied to the coordination efforts at local level (ex. Local platforms), as well as supranational level.

articles.title: CAR: République centrafricaine - Mise à jour sur la sécurité alimentaire : L’insécurité prolonge des résultats de Crise (Phase 3 de l’IPC) au début de la soudure au sud du pays, Avril - Septembre 2026
articles.description:
Country: Central African Republic
Source: Famine Early Warning System Network

Please refer to the attached file.

Messages clé

  • Entre avril et septembre 2026, des résultats de Crise (Phase 3 de l’IPC) devraient persister dans le nord-est, sud-est et nord-ouest du pays, dans les préfectures les plus touchées par l’insécurité et l’enclavement. L’insécurité persistante continuera de restreindre l’accès aux champs, aux opportunités de revenus et aux principaux axes commerciaux. Une dépendance aux marchés des ménages pauvres, combinée à l’épuisement de leurs stocks ainsi qu’à des prix élevés des denrées alimentaires et à des opportunités de revenus saisonnièrement faibles, est attendue pendant la période de soudure au nord, de juillet à septembre. La dégradation de l’accès à la nourriture est anticipée en particulier dans les zones affectées par l’insécurité et l’enclavement, contribuant à une érosion progressive des moyens d’existence des ménages pauvres et déplacés.
  • Dans les régions de Ouaka, Basse‑Kotto, Ouham-Fafa, et Mbomou, des résultats de Crise (Phase 3 de l’IPC) sont attendus entre avril et juin pendant la soudure en raison de l’épuisement des stocks, d’une forte dépendance aux marchés, de revenus saisonniers limités et des prix élevés. À partir de juillet, l’arrivée des récoltes permettra une amélioration vers des résultats de Stress (Phase 2 de l’IPC) dans la plupart des zones, sauf dans les localités enclavées ou affectées par l’insécurité persistante.
  • Dans les préfectures moins impactées par l’insécurité dans le centre et sud, des résultats de Stress (Phase 2 de l’IPC) sont attendus jusqu’en septembre 2026. Le fonctionnement relativement meilleur des marchés et le démarrage des opportunités saisonnières de main‑d’œuvre agricole au début de la saison des pluies devraient atténuer l’érosion des moyens d’existence des ménages pauvres, sans toutefois couvrir pleinement les dépenses non alimentaires essentielles. Les ménages pauvres, en particulier les ménages hôtes et les déplacés urbains à Bangui, continueront de faire face à un pouvoir d’achat réduit, avec des hausses saisonnières des prix.

Ce rapport fournit une mise à jour des perspectives sur la sécurité alimentaire pour février 2026 à septembre 2026 et des messages clés de mars 2026. L’analyse est basée sur les informations disponibles au 28 avril 2026.

articles.title: South Sudan: Food Security Outlook Update: Conflict disrupts humanitarian access, sustaining Emergency and risk of Famine in Nasir, April - September 2026
articles.description:
Country: South Sudan
Source: Famine Early Warning System Network

Please refer to the attached file.

Key Messages

  • Emergency (IPC Phase 4) outcomes are expected to be widespread in 34 counties through September, driven by conflict escalation, large-scale displacement, anticipated flooding, deteriorating economy, high food prices, and restricted humanitarian access (particularly in Jonglei, Upper Nile, and the Warrap-Unity border region). Areas of highest concern are Akobo, Nyirol, Uror, and Fangak (Jonglei), and Nasir and Ulang (Upper Nile) where some populations are expected to face Catastrophe (IPC Phase 5) due to the scale of disruption to livelihoods and humanitarian access amid conflict.
  • The prevalence of acute malnutrition is expected to further worsen during the upcoming rainy season. Up to 11 counties in Jonglei, Upper Nile, Unity, and Abyei are expected to face Extremely Critical levels of Global Acute Malnutrition (GAM>30 percent) by June amid conflict-driven disruptions and access to health, nutrition, and water, sanitation, and hygiene services, rainy season conditions, and increasingly severe disease burden.
  • Humanitarians continue to face critical access challenges, particularly in Jonglei and Upper Nile. While planned assistance levels will be scaled up during the lean season response between May-August, targeting on average 1.6 million people (less than 20 percent of those in need), conflict and flooding are likely to disrupt delivery. Assistance is expected to mitigate more severe outcomes in only five counties through May, declining to just three counties (in Unity) in June-September.
  • There is a credible risk of Famine (IPC Phase 5) in Akobo and Nyirol (Jonglei), and Nasir and Ulang (Upper Nile), with particular concern for inaccessible areas hosting large numbers of internally displaced populations (IDPs), such as Nyatim and Chuil of Nyirol. IDPs have fled violence and destruction often multiple times, lost income and food sources, and are most exposed to surging disease outbreaks in overcrowded conditions. If conflict escalates and isolates populations, including local food sources and food aid, for a prolonged period, then Famine (IPC Phase 5) would likely occur.

This report provides an update to the February to September 2026 Food Security Outlook and March 2026 Key Message Update. The analysis is based on information available as of April 30, 2026.

articles.title: Caribbean regional workshop on DELTA Resilience System and the Sendai Framework Monitor
articles.description: To support countries in this endeavor, the United Nations Office for Disaster Risk Reduction (UNDRR), in collaboration with partners, has developed the Disaster & Hazardous Events, Losses and Damages Tracking & Analysis (DELTA Resilience) system.

articles.title: UNDRR 2025 Annual Report
articles.description: In 2025, we marked the 10-year anniversary of the Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction and its goal of significantly reducing disaster risk and losses by 2030. This report captures the progress, partnerships, and impact of UNDRR's work throughout the year.

articles.title: Quelimane, Mozambique puts urban resilience into practice with a new DRR action plan
articles.description: The United Nations Office for Disaster Risk Reduction (UNDRR), in collaboration with the Municipality of Quelimane, organised an event to integrate technical DRR needs into municipal planning for Quelimane, a major seaport city in Mozambique’.

articles.title: Nile Basin countries advance early warning systems through integrated risk assessment and forecasting
articles.description: Countries across the Nile Basin are strengthening their capacity to anticipate and respond to climate-related hazards, as experts convened for a four-day technical workshop to advance multi-hazard risk assessment and flood early warning systems

articles.title: UNDRR expands Sendai Stakeholder Engagement Mechanism to LGBTIQ+ Persons constituency
articles.description: UNDRR has formally added an LGBTIQ+ Persons constituency to its Stakeholder Engagement Mechanism to strengthen inclusive disaster risk reduction policy and participation.
